context: Your AI assistant generated a complete authentication module in 30 seconds. It compiles. The tests pass.
question: Do you merge it?

optionA.label: Merge it — it works
optionA.consequence: AI-generated code carries a 40% rate of security flaws. A hardcoded secret and two SQL injections go undetected.

optionB.label: Run Sonar first
optionB.consequence: 3 vulnerabilities caught before merge, including a hardcoded API key. Clean PR.

context: Your team adopted an AI coding assistant 3 months ago. Velocity doubled. No Quality Gate has been configured in CI/CD.
question: Is that fine?

optionA.label: Velocity is up — don't add friction
optionA.consequence: Six months later: 340 open issues. Technical debt estimated at 47 days. Nobody wants to touch the codebase.

optionB.label: Set up Quality Gates from day one
optionB.consequence: AI productivity stays high. Every PR is automatically checked. Quality scales with the team.

context: Sprint ends Friday. Two hours left. The feature works, but Sonar reports 12 issues — including one BLOCKER.
question: Do you ignore the warnings?

optionA.label: Disable SonarLint, ship the feature
optionA.consequence: Tech debt compounds. Three months later, that BLOCKER becomes a production incident.

optionB.label: Fix the critical issues now
optionB.consequence: 45 minutes of focused work. Zero critical issues at release. The sprint ends clean.

context: A PR comes in with a new third-party API integration. The key is hardcoded in the source — 'just for testing', says the author.
question: Do you approve the PR?

optionA.label: Approve — we'll clean it up later
optionA.consequence: The key lands in git history. A public fork leaks it six weeks later. Credential rotation takes a full day.

optionB.label: Block it — no secrets in code, ever
optionB.consequence: 20 minutes to fix properly with a secrets manager. Zero exposure. The habit sticks across the team.

context: Sonar flags a dependency with a CVE score of 8.1. Fixing it requires a major version upgrade — roughly two days of work.
question: How do you handle it?

optionA.label: Snooze it — add it to the backlog
optionA.consequence: The vulnerability is exploited 10 weeks later. Incident response costs 3 weeks, one data breach, and a GDPR fine.

optionB.label: Prioritize the migration now
optionB.consequence: Two days of focused work. Full compliance maintained. No breach, no fine, no emergency.

context: You find a function with a cyclomatic complexity of 47, spanning 500 lines, with zero test coverage. It's been in production for 3 years.
question: What do you do?

optionA.label: Don't touch it — if it works, don't fix it
optionA.consequence: A new teammate modifies one line. A regression ships to production. It takes a week to trace it back.

optionB.label: Refactor it progressively
optionB.consequence: Complexity drops to 9. Two hidden bugs surface and are fixed before they ever reach production.

context: Coverage is at 78%. Adding tests for the new feature would take 2 extra hours the team feels it doesn't have today.
question: Do you ship without tests?

optionA.label: Ship now, write tests later
optionA.consequence: 'Later' never comes. Coverage drifts to 45% over 6 months. Regressions become routine. Confidence collapses.

optionB.label: Negotiate scope, protect the coverage gate
optionB.consequence: One less sub-feature this sprint. Zero regressions next quarter. The Quality Gate holds.

context: A Stack Overflow snippet from 2019 solves your problem perfectly. It has 847 upvotes and looks clean.
question: Do you use it as-is?

optionA.label: Copy-paste — it's community-validated
optionA.consequence: The snippet uses MD5 for password hashing and a deprecated crypto function with a known CVE. It ships.

optionB.label: Review it before using it
optionB.consequence: 10 minutes of review. You switch to bcrypt. Sonar confirms: no vulnerability introduced.
